title: Decision Trees & Ensembles Quiz
description: Test your knowledge on Trees, Random Forests, Bagging, and Boosting algorithms.

questions.question: What is the primary vulnerability of a single, deeply grown Decision Tree?

options.text: It requires perfect linear normalization of the input data.
It takes weeks to compile.
It is inherently biased against floating-point numbers.
It is extremely prone to severe overfitting, capturing noise and memorizing the training data perfectly while failing on new data.

questions.question: What broad concept describes combining the independent predictions of multiple weaker ML models to produce a single, stronger, more robust prediction?

options.text: Unsupervised Clustering
Deep Learning Processing
Ensemble Learning
Data Normalization

questions.question: How does a 'Random Forest' algorithm fundamentally combat the overfitting found in single decision trees?

options.text: By employing 'Bagging'—training hundreds of parallel trees on random subsets of rows and features, and forcing them to democratically vote on the final prediction.
By executing a sequential process analyzing the errors of previous iterations.
By using raw Calculus to calculate the partial derivatives.
By rejecting null hypothesis tests during node splits.

questions.question: How does 'Boosting' (as seen in XGBoost) fundamentally differ from 'Bagging' (as seen in Random Forests)?

options.text: Boosting trains trees completely in parallel simultaneously, while Bagging trains sequentially.
Boosting only builds a single giant tree.
Boosting trains trees *sequentially*, where each subsequent weak model specifically attempts to correct the mathematical errors (residuals) made by the previous model in the chain.
Boosting is an Unsupervised mechanism, Bagging is Supervised.

questions.question: Which of these algorithms would likely perform best out-of-the-box on a complex Tabular dataset (like CSV spreadsheets) rather than unstructured data like images?

options.text: Convolutional Neural Network
Transformer Architecture (LLM)
XGBoost (Extreme Gradient Boosting)
Recurrent Neural Network (LSTM)
